Common Cold.description: The common cold is a viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is usually harmless and resolves within 7-10 days.
causes: Spread through airborne droplets or direct contact
prevention: Wash hands frequently with soap and water
Avoid touching face with unwashed hands
Maintain a healthy immune system with proper nutrition
home_remedies: Stay well hydrated — drink water, warm soups, and herbal teas
Gargle with warm salt water for sore throat
Use a humidifier to add moisture to the air
Try honey and lemon in warm water
Consume vitamin C-rich foods like oranges and lemons
general_advice: Over-the-counter decongestants may help relieve nasal congestion
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain and fever relief
Saline nasal spray can help with stuffiness
Throat lozenges for sore throat relief
Avoid antibiotics — they don't work against viruses
when_to_see_doctor: Fever above 101.3°F (38.5°C) lasting more than 3 days
Symptoms that worsen after initial improvement
clinical_treatments: NSAIDs or acetaminophen for fever and pain

Flu.description: Influenza (flu) is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. It can range from mild to severe and can lead to serious complications, especially in high-risk groups.
common_symptoms: Severe body aches and muscle pain
risk_factors: Living in nursing homes or long-term care facilities
prevention: Avoid close contact with sick people
Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing
home_remedies: Drink plenty of fluids — water, broth, and electrolyte drinks
Use a humidifier for congestion relief
Gargle with salt water for sore throat
Take warm baths to reduce fever and body aches
Eat light, nutritious meals like chicken soup
general_advice: Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and pain
Antiviral medications (oseltamivir) if prescribed within 48 hours of onset
Stay home for at least 24 hours after fever subsides
Monitor symptoms for signs of complications
Avoid aspirin in children and teenagers
when_to_see_doctor: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
Flu-like symptoms that improve then return with fever and worse cough
High fever lasting more than 3 days
clinical_treatments: Antiviral drugs (e.g., Oseltamivir/Tamiflu) if diagnosed early

Pneumonia.description: Pneumonia is an infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs. The air sacs may fill with fluid or pus, causing cough with phlegm, fever, chills, and difficulty breathing.
causes: Aspiration of food, liquid, or vomit
common_symptoms: Chest pain when breathing or coughing
home_remedies: Stay well hydrated with warm fluids
Use a humidifier to ease breathing
Drink warm water with honey and turmeric
Gentle breathing exercises when feeling better
general_advice: Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics if bacterial
Over-the-counter fever reducers as needed
Avoid cough suppressants unless directed by doctor
Gradually increase activity as you recover
Follow-up chest X-ray may be recommended
when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate medical attention for pneumonia symptoms
clinical_treatments: Antibiotics (e.g., Azithromycin, Amoxicillin) for bacterial pneumonia

Diabetes.description: Diabetes is a chronic metabolic disease characterized by elevated blood sugar levels. Over time, uncontrolled diabetes can lead to serious damage to the heart, blood vessels, eyes, kidneys, and nerves.
causes: Type 1: Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing cells
Type 2: Insulin resistance and insufficient insulin production
prevention: Exercise regularly (at least 150 minutes per week)
Limit refined carbohydrates and sugary drinks
home_remedies: Eat fiber-rich foods like whole grains, vegetables, and legumes
Manage stress through yoga and meditation
Monitor portion sizes and eat at regular intervals
general_advice: Monitor blood sugar levels as advised by your doctor
Take prescribed medications regularly
Maintain a food diary
Wear medical identification
Get regular eye, foot, and kidney examinations
Limit alcohol consumption
when_to_see_doctor: Blood sugar levels consistently above normal range
Symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis (fruity breath, nausea, confusion)

Hypertension.description: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is a condition where the force of blood against artery walls is consistently too high. It is often called the 'silent killer' because it typically has no symptoms but can lead to serious health problems.
causes: Essential/primary hypertension (no identifiable cause, develops over years)
Secondary hypertension from kidney disease, thyroid problems, or medications
prevention: Reduce sodium intake (less than 2,300 mg per day)
home_remedies: Reduce salt intake and eat potassium-rich foods (bananas, spinach)
Practice deep breathing exercises and meditation
Try the DASH diet (rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains)
general_advice: Monitor blood pressure at home regularly
Take prescribed medications consistently
Reduce stress through relaxation techniques
Limit processed and fast foods
Maintain a healthy BMI
Avoid excessive alcohol consumption
when_to_see_doctor: Blood pressure reading above 180/120 mmHg (hypertensive crisis)

Migraine.description: Migraine is a neurological condition characterized by intense, debilitating headaches often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. Attacks can last from hours to days.
causes: Changes in brainstem interactions with the trigeminal nerve
common_symptoms: Intense throbbing or pulsating headache (usually one-sided)
risk_factors: Female sex (3 times more common in women)
prevention: Keep a migraine diary to track patterns
home_remedies: Rest in a dark, quiet room
Apply cold compress to forehead or neck
Try peppermint or lavender essential oil
general_advice: Over-the-counter pain relievers (ibuprofen, acetaminophen) at onset
Triptans may be prescribed for moderate to severe migraines
Anti-nausea medications if needed
Preventive medications for frequent migraines
Avoid overuse of pain medications
when_to_see_doctor: Headache with fever, stiff neck, or rash
Weakness, numbness, or vision loss with headache
clinical_treatments: Preventive medications (beta blockers, antidepressants, or anti-seizure drugs)

Dengue.description: Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral infection causing a severe flu-like illness. It is transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes and can develop into severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever), which can be life-threatening.
causes: Transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes
Cannot spread directly from person to person
common_symptoms: Skin rash (appears 2-5 days after fever onset)
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to tropical/subtropical areas
prevention: Wear long-sleeved shirts and long pants
Eliminate standing water around your home
Get dengue vaccine if available in your area
home_remedies: Drink plenty of fluids — water, coconut water, and ORS
Papaya leaf extract (shown to help increase platelet count)
Drink fresh pomegranate or kiwi juice
general_advice: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) for fever and pain
AVOID aspirin and ibuprofen (increases bleeding risk)
Monitor platelet count regularly
Watch for warning signs of severe dengue
IV fluids may be needed in severe cases
clinical_treatments: Blood transfusion in severe hemorrhagic cases

Malaria.description: Malaria is a life-threatening mosquito-borne disease caused by Plasmodium parasites. It is transmitted through the bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes and causes recurring episodes of fever and chills.
causes: Plasmodium parasites (P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. malariae, P. ovale)
Can also be transmitted through blood transfusion or shared needles
common_symptoms: Periodic high fever with chills and sweating
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions
Poverty and lack of healthcare access
prevention: Take antimalarial prophylaxis when traveling to endemic areas
home_remedies: Stay well hydrated with water and electrolyte solutions
Consume citrus fruits for vitamin C
Ginger tea to help with nausea
Cinnamon tea may help reduce symptoms
general_advice: Antimalarial medications as prescribed by doctor are essential
Complete the full course of treatment
Monitor for complications
Blood tests needed to confirm diagnosis and monitor treatment
Preventive treatment may be needed for travelers
when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate medical attention if malaria is suspected
High fever with chills after visiting endemic area
clinical_treatments: Mefloquine or Quinine sulfate depending on resistance

Typhoid.description: Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi. It spreads through contaminated food and water and is common in areas with poor sanitation. Without treatment, it can be life-threatening.
causes: Carriers who don't show symptoms can spread the bacteria
risk_factors: Traveling to endemic areas (South Asia, Africa)
Eating street food in endemic areas
prevention: Get typhoid vaccine before traveling to endemic areas
Drink only bottled or boiled water
Avoid raw fruits and vegetables unless peeled yourself
Avoid ice from questionable water sources
home_remedies: Drink plenty of fluids (boiled water, fresh juices, coconut water)
Eat easily digestible foods (rice porridge, boiled vegetables)
Banana and apple for energy and nutrition
general_advice: Antibiotics are essential — complete the full course as prescribed
Fluid replacement therapy if dehydrated
Bland, high-calorie diet recommended
Monitor for intestinal complications
Stool cultures to confirm when no longer a carrier
clinical_treatments: Surgery in case of intestinal perforation

Tuberculosis.description: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis that primarily affects the lungs but can affect other parts of the body. It spreads through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes.
causes: Airborne transmission through coughing, sneezing, or talking
Close prolonged contact with infected individuals
home_remedies: Nutritious diet rich in protein, vitamins, and minerals
Green tea for its antioxidant benefits
Fresh fruit juices for vitamin C
general_advice: TB requires prescribed antibiotics for 6-9 months minimum
NEVER stop medication early even if feeling better
Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) may be recommended
Regular follow-up tests to monitor progress
Multidrug-resistant TB requires specialized treatment
when_to_see_doctor: Unexplained weight loss and night sweats
Exposure to someone with active TB
clinical_treatments: Long-term antibiotic regimen (e.g., Isoniazid, Rifampin, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide) for 6-9 months

Asthma.description: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition in which the airways become inflamed, narrow, and swell, producing extra mucus. This makes breathing difficult and triggers coughing, wheezing, and shortness of breath.
causes: Environmental allergens (dust mites, pollen, pet dander)
common_symptoms: Chronic cough (especially at night or early morning)
Difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies
home_remedies: Practice breathing exercises (pursed lip and diaphragmatic breathing)
Drink warm fluids to thin mucus
Coffee or caffeinated tea (caffeine is a mild bronchodilator)
general_advice: Use prescribed inhalers (rescue and controller) as directed
Keep rescue inhaler accessible at all times
Follow your asthma action plan
Use a peak flow meter to monitor lung function
Take controller medications daily, not just during attacks
Avoid known allergens and irritants
when_to_see_doctor: Difficulty speaking or walking due to breathlessness
Peak flow readings in the red zone
Frequent nighttime awakenings due to asthma
clinical_treatments: Short-acting beta agonists (e.g., Albuterol) for rescue

Bronchitis.description: Bronchitis is an inflammation of the lining of the bronchial tubes, which carry air to and from the lungs. Acute bronchitis is common and often develops from a cold or respiratory infection.
causes: Viruses (same viruses that cause colds and flu)
home_remedies: Use a humidifier to moisten the air
Drink warm liquids (herbal tea, broth, warm water with honey)
Gargle with salt water for throat relief
Avoid dairy products which may thicken mucus
general_advice: Most acute bronchitis resolves on its own in 1-3 weeks
Cough suppressants for nighttime relief
Expectorants to help thin mucus
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and discomfort
Avoid antibiotics unless bacterial infection is confirmed
when_to_see_doctor: Fever above 100.4°F (38°C) for more than 3 days
clinical_treatments: Antibiotics (only if a bacterial infection is identified)

Gastroenteritis.description: Gastroenteritis (stomach flu) is an inflammation of the stomach and intestines, typically resulting from bacterial or viral infection. It causes watery diarrhea, abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, and sometimes fever.
prevention: Avoid sharing utensils with sick persons
home_remedies: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) to prevent dehydration
Clear fluids — water, broth, and coconut water
Avoid dairy, caffeine, and spicy foods
general_advice: Focus on hydration — small, frequent sips
Electrolyte replacement drinks
Anti-diarrheal medications for adults if needed
Avoid solid foods until vomiting stops
Gradually reintroduce bland foods
Rest as much as possible
when_to_see_doctor: Signs of dehydration (dry mouth, dark urine, dizziness)
Inability to keep fluids down for 24 hours
clinical_treatments: Anti-diarrheal medication (e.g., Loperamide) in specific cases

Urinary Tract Infection.description: A urinary tract infection (UTI) is an infection in any part of the urinary system — kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra. Most infections involve the lower urinary tract (bladder and urethra).
causes: Bacteria (usually E. coli) entering the urinary tract
common_symptoms: Passing small amounts of urine frequently
prevention: Change birth control method if causing recurring UTIs
Don't hold urine for long periods
home_remedies: Drink plenty of water (8-10 glasses daily)
Cranberry juice or supplements (may help prevent recurrence)
Apply heating pad to abdomen for pain
Vitamin C-rich foods to acidify urine
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods
general_advice: Antibiotics are typically needed for bacterial UTIs
Complete the full course of antibiotics
Pain relievers for discomfort
Phenazopyridine for urinary pain (turns urine orange)
Drink plenty of fluids during treatment
when_to_see_doctor: Symptoms of UTI should always be evaluated by a doctor
Back pain or fever (may indicate kidney infection)
Recurring UTIs (3 or more per year)

Anemia.description: Anemia is a condition where you lack enough healthy red blood cells to carry adequate oxygen to your body's tissues. It can make you feel tired, weak, and short of breath.
risk_factors: Poor diet lacking iron, B12, or folate
prevention: Eat iron-rich foods (spinach, red meat, beans, fortified cereals)
Consume vitamin C to enhance iron absorption
Regular health checkups and blood tests
home_remedies: Increase iron-rich foods in diet (spinach, lentils, tofu, red meat)
Eat vitamin C-rich foods alongside iron foods for better absorption
Beetroot juice for natural iron boost
Pomegranate juice for iron and vitamin C
Dates and raisins as daily snacks
general_advice: Iron supplements if recommended by doctor
B12 injections for pernicious anemia
Folic acid supplements if needed
Treat underlying causes
Avoid tea and coffee with meals (inhibit iron absorption)
Regular blood count monitoring
when_to_see_doctor: Shortness of breath during normal activities
clinical_treatments: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) in severe chronic cases

Arthritis.description: Arthritis is inflammation of one or more joints, causing pain, swelling, and stiffness that typically worsen with age. The most common types are osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis.
causes: Osteoarthritis: wear and tear of joint cartilage
Rheumatoid arthritis: immune system attacks joint lining
risk_factors: Gender (women more likely to develop RA)
prevention: Use proper body mechanics when lifting
home_remedies: Cold packs for swollen, inflamed joints
Epsom salt bath for pain relief
Massage with warm sesame or coconut oil
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce joint stress
general_advice: Over-the-counter NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain
Topical pain relievers (creams, patches)
Physical therapy for joint mobility
Assistive devices if needed
Disease-modifying drugs for rheumatoid arthritis
Regular low-impact exercise
when_to_see_doctor: Joint pain lasting more than 3 days
Redness and warmth in joint area
Sudden intense joint pain (may indicate gout)
clinical_treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) like Methotrexate for RA

Chicken Pox.description: Chicken pox (varicella) is a highly contagious viral infection causing an itchy, blister-like rash. It mainly affects children but can occur at any age and is usually more severe in adults.
causes: Spread through direct contact with rash
Airborne transmission through coughing and sneezing
Highly contagious from 1-2 days before rash appears until all blisters have crusted
common_symptoms: Rash progresses from red spots to blisters to crusted scabs
risk_factors: Never had chicken pox or vaccination
prevention: Isolate infected persons until all blisters have crusted
home_remedies: Keep nails trimmed short to prevent scratching
Eat soft, bland foods if mouth sores present
general_advice: Acetaminophen for fever (NOT aspirin — risk of Reye's syndrome)
Antihistamines for itching
Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be prescribed for adults or severe cases
Keep rash clean and dry
Avoid scratching to prevent scarring and infection
when_to_see_doctor: High fever (above 102°F/39°C) lasting more than 4 days
Rash becoming warm, tender, or producing pus (secondary infection)
clinical_treatments: Antiviral medications (e.g., Acyclovir) for high-risk patients
Calamine lotion and colloidal oatmeal baths

Jaundice.description: Jaundice is a condition where the skin and whites of the eyes turn yellow due to elevated bilirubin levels in the blood. It is not a disease itself but a sign of an underlying condition affecting the liver, bile ducts, or blood.
causes: Hemolytic anemia (rapid breakdown of red blood cells)
prevention: Get vaccinated for hepatitis A and B
Be cautious with medications that affect the liver
home_remedies: Drink sugarcane juice for liver support
Lemon juice in warm water every morning
Tomato juice with a pinch of salt and pepper
Avoid fatty, oily, and spicy foods
Eat light meals — rice, boiled vegetables, fruits
general_advice: Treatment depends on the underlying cause
Rest is important for recovery
Avoid alcohol completely
Medications as prescribed for the underlying condition
Phototherapy for newborn jaundice
Surgery may be needed for gallstone-related jaundice
when_to_see_doctor: Any yellowing of skin or eyes should be evaluated
Abdominal pain especially in the upper right area
clinical_treatments: Treatment targets the underlying cause (e.g., antiviral meds for Hepatitis)
Surgical removal of gallstones if they are the cause
Liver transplant in cases of severe liver failure

Hepatitis.description: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver, commonly caused by viral infections (A, B, C, D, E). It can be acute or chronic and may lead to liver scarring (cirrhosis), liver failure, or liver cancer.
causes: Hepatitis B: body fluids (blood, sexual contact)
prevention: Don't share needles or personal items
home_remedies: Green leafy vegetables for liver health
general_advice: Antiviral medications for hepatitis B and C
Hepatitis A typically resolves on its own
Regular liver function monitoring
Complete vaccination series
Screen sexual partners
Avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic drugs
when_to_see_doctor: Any symptoms of hepatitis should be evaluated
Dark urine or very pale stools
clinical_treatments: Antiviral medications (e.g., Entecavir, Tenofovir for Hep B; Sofosbuvir for Hep C)
Liver transplantation for end-stage liver disease

Allergic Rhinitis.description: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever) is an allergic response to allergens like pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or mold. It causes cold-like symptoms such as sneezing, congestion, and itchy eyes.
causes: Immune system overreaction to harmless airborne particles
common_symptoms: Itchy eyes, nose, mouth, or throat
prevention: Monitor pollen counts and stay indoors when high
Keep windows closed during allergy season
Wash bedding in hot water weekly
Vacuum regularly with HEPA filter vacuum
home_remedies: Local honey (may help with pollen allergies)
general_advice: Antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) for symptom relief
Nasal corticosteroid sprays for persistent symptoms
Decongestants for short-term use
Allergy immunotherapy for severe cases
Eye drops for itchy, watery eyes
when_to_see_doctor: Symptoms not controlled with OTC medications
Symptoms affecting sleep or daily activities

Sinusitis.description: Sinusitis is inflammation or swelling of the tissue lining the sinuses. When sinuses become blocked and filled with fluid, germs can grow and cause infection. It can be acute (lasting less than 4 weeks) or chronic.
common_symptoms: Reduced sense of smell and taste
home_remedies: Steam inhalation with eucalyptus or peppermint
Warm compresses on face for pain relief
Spicy foods to help clear sinuses
Apple cider vinegar in warm water
general_advice: Decongestant nasal sprays (limit to 3 days)
Saline sprays for regular use
Pain relievers for facial pain and headache
Antibiotics if bacterial infection confirmed
Nasal corticosteroid sprays for chronic sinusitis
Surgery may be needed for chronic cases
clinical_treatments: Antibiotics (e.g., Amoxicillin) if bacterial infection is suspected

Vertigo.description: Vertigo is the sensation that you or your surroundings are spinning or moving when there is no actual movement. It is a symptom of various conditions, most commonly related to the inner ear.
causes: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV) — most common
prevention: Sit down immediately if feeling dizzy
Walk with a cane if unsteady
home_remedies: Epley maneuver for BPPV (ask doctor to demonstrate)
general_advice: Meclizine or dimenhydrinate for motion sickness/nausea
Vestibular rehabilitation therapy
Medications for Meniere's disease
Physical therapy exercises
Avoid rapid position changes
when_to_see_doctor: Vertigo with headache, fever, or hearing loss
Chest pain or rapid heartbeat with vertigo

Acne.description: Acne is a skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells. It commonly appears on the face, forehead, chest, upper back, and shoulders.
common_symptoms: Large painful lumps under the skin (nodules)
risk_factors: Greasy or oily substances on skin
prevention: Wash face twice daily with gentle cleanser
Don't touch or pick at your face
home_remedies: Tea tree oil (diluted) as spot treatment
Stay hydrated and eat a balanced diet
general_advice: Benzoyl peroxide for killing acne bacteria
Salicylic acid for unclogging pores
Retinoids (adapalene) for preventing clogged pores
Don't pop or squeeze pimples
Be patient — acne treatments take 6-8 weeks to show results
Use oil-free, non-comedogenic moisturizer and sunscreen
when_to_see_doctor: Over-the-counter products not helping after 2-3 months
Acne leaving scars or dark spots
Acne affecting self-esteem or mental health
Acne in women with other signs of hormonal imbalance

Eczema.description: Eczema (atopic dermatitis) is a chronic skin condition that makes skin red, inflamed, itchy, and cracked. It is common in children but can occur at any age and tends to flare periodically.
common_symptoms: Small raised bumps that may leak fluid
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, allergies, or asthma
prevention: Moisturize skin at least twice daily
Use a humidifier in dry weather
home_remedies: Apply coconut oil to moisturize affected areas
Sunflower seed oil to strengthen skin barrier
Keep nails short to prevent scratching damage
general_advice: Emollients and moisturizers as base treatment
Topical corticosteroids for flares
Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus)
Antihistamines for nighttime itching
Avoid harsh soaps and hot water
Phototherapy for severe cases
when_to_see_doctor: Eczema not responding to home care
Signs of skin infection (pus, fever, redness)
clinical_treatments: Injectable biologics (e.g., Dupilumab) for severe cases

Psoriasis.description: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition that causes the rapid buildup of skin cells, resulting in thick, silvery scales and itchy, dry, red patches that can be painful.
causes: Overactive immune system (T cells attack healthy skin cells)
common_symptoms: Red patches covered with silvery scales
Dry, cracked skin that may bleed
Patches can range from small spots to large areas
prevention: Limit sun exposure while getting moderate amounts
home_remedies: Regular moisturizing with thick creams or ointments
Warm baths with Epsom salts, oatmeal, or Dead Sea salts
Aloe vera gel directly on patches
Tea tree oil (diluted) for scalp psoriasis
general_advice: Topical corticosteroids for mild to moderate psoriasis
Vitamin D analogues (calcipotriol)
Coal tar preparations
Phototherapy (UV light treatment)
Systemic medications for severe cases
Biologic drugs for moderate to severe psoriasis
when_to_see_doctor: Psoriasis covering large areas of the body
Signs of infection in psoriasis patches

Food Poisoning.description: Food poisoning is an illness caused by eating contaminated food. Infectious organisms — bacteria, viruses, and parasites — or their toxins are the most common causes.
risk_factors: Elderly, young children, and pregnant women
prevention: Cook meat to proper internal temperatures
When in doubt, throw it out
home_remedies: Stay hydrated with water, clear broth, and ORS
BRAT diet when ready to eat (Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast)
Rest the stomach — avoid eating for a few hours
general_advice: Most food poisoning resolves in 1-3 days
Anti-diarrheal medications may help (adults only)
Anti-nausea medications if prescribed
Electrolyte replacement solutions
Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol, and fatty foods until recovered
Antibiotics only if bacterial infection is confirmed
when_to_see_doctor: Inability to keep any fluids down
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Appendicitis.description: Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix, a small pouch attached to the large intestine. It is a medical emergency that usually requires surgical removal of the appendix (appendectomy).
common_symptoms: Sudden pain beginning around the navel and shifting to lower right abdomen
prevention: No proven method to prevent appendicitis
Eat plenty of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
home_remedies: DO NOT attempt to treat appendicitis at home
DO NOT take laxatives or pain medications before diagnosis
DO NOT eat or drink anything if appendicitis is suspected
Apply ice pack (not heat) to abdomen for comfort while seeking medical help
general_advice: Appendicitis is a medical emergency requiring prompt treatment
Surgery (appendectomy) is the standard treatment
Laparoscopic surgery is preferred when possible
Antibiotics may be given before and after surgery
Recovery usually takes 1-3 weeks
Delay in treatment can cause the appendix to rupture
when_to_see_doctor: Sudden severe abdominal pain in the right lower area
Pain that worsens with movement, coughing, or sneezing
Inability to pass gas with abdominal swelling
DO NOT wait — ruptured appendix is life-threatening
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Kidney Stones.description: Kidney stones are hard deposits made of minerals and salts that form inside the kidneys. They can affect any part of the urinary tract. Passing stones can be extremely painful but usually doesn't cause permanent damage.
causes: Concentrated urine allowing minerals to crystallize
common_symptoms: Severe pain in the side and back below the ribs
Pain radiating to lower abdomen and groin
Fever and chills if infection present
risk_factors: Family or personal history of kidney stones
prevention: Drink plenty of water throughout the day (2-3 liters)
Eat calcium-rich foods (but be cautious with supplements)
Avoid high-oxalate foods if prone to oxalate stones
home_remedies: Drink lots of water (aim for 2-3 liters daily)
Lemon juice (citrate helps prevent stone formation)
Stay active to help pass small stones
Reduce salt and processed food intake
general_advice: Small stones (less than 5mm) may pass on their own
Pain relievers (ibuprofen) for stone-passing pain
Alpha-blockers may help relax ureter muscles
Lithotripsy (sound waves) for larger stones
Surgical removal for very large stones
Strain urine to catch passed stones for analysis
when_to_see_doctor: Pain accompanied by nausea and vomiting
Pain with fever and chills (may indicate infection)
Stone too large to pass naturally
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Hypothyroidism.description: Hypothyroidism is a condition where the thyroid gland doesn't produce enough thyroid hormones. This slows metabolism and can affect many body systems, causing fatigue, weight gain, and cold sensitivity.
risk_factors: Radiation to neck or upper chest
prevention: Regular thyroid function tests if at risk
home_remedies: Eat iodine-rich foods (seaweed, fish, dairy, eggs)
Include selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts, tuna, eggs)
Coconut oil in cooking (may support thyroid function)
Manage stress with yoga and meditation
Avoid processed foods and refined sugars
general_advice: Levothyroxine (synthetic thyroid hormone) is the standard treatment
Take medication on an empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast
Regular blood tests to monitor thyroid levels
Don't change dosage without doctor's approval
Avoid taking calcium or iron supplements near thyroid medication
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Hyperthyroidism.description: Hyperthyroidism is a condition where the thyroid gland produces too much thyroid hormone, accelerating the body's metabolism. This can cause rapid heartbeat, weight loss, nervousness, and irritability.
prevention: Don't smoke (increases risk of Graves' disease)
home_remedies: Anti-inflammatory foods (berries, leafy greens, omega-3 rich fish)
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods for bone health
Lemon balm tea (may help reduce thyroid hormone)
Manage stress with meditation and yoga
general_advice: Anti-thyroid medications (methimazole, propylthiouracil)
Beta-blockers for rapid heartbeat and tremors
Radioactive iodine therapy
Thyroid surgery in some cases
Regular monitoring of thyroid levels
Calcium and vitamin D supplements for bone health
when_to_see_doctor: Unexplained weight loss despite good appetite
Thyroid storm symptoms (fever, rapid pulse, confusion) — emergency
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

GERD.description: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a chronic digestive condition where stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus. This acid reflux can irritate the esophageal lining and cause heartburn.
common_symptoms: Sensation of a lump in throat
risk_factors: Eating large meals late at night
prevention: Don't lie down after eating (wait 2-3 hours)
Don't wear tight-fitting clothing around abdomen
home_remedies: Baking soda in water (temporary relief only)
Avoid trigger foods (spicy, fatty, acidic, coffee, chocolate)
general_advice: Antacids for occasional relief
H2 receptor blockers (famotidine)
Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole) for frequent symptoms
Lifestyle modifications are essential
Avoid long-term antacid overuse
Surgery (fundoplication) for severe, unresponsive cases
when_to_see_doctor: Heartburn more than twice a week
Symptoms not improving with OTC medications
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Conjunctivitis.description: Conjunctivitis (pink eye) is inflammation or infection of the transparent membrane (conjunctiva) that lines the eyelid and covers the white part of the eyeball. It makes eyes red, itchy, and sometimes produces discharge.
common_symptoms: Redness in one or both eyes
prevention: Don't share personal items (towels, eye makeup)
home_remedies: Warm or cool compresses on closed eyes
Clean discharge with clean, damp cloth
Rose water drops for soothing (use only sterile solutions)
Cold chamomile tea bags on closed eyes
Avoid wearing contact lenses until resolved
general_advice: Viral conjunctivitis usually resolves on its own (1-2 weeks)
Antibiotic eye drops for bacterial conjunctivitis
Antihistamine or anti-inflammatory drops for allergic conjunctivitis
Don't wear contact lenses until infection clears
Replace eye makeup and lens case after infection
when_to_see_doctor: Blurred vision that doesn't improve with blinking
Symptoms not improving after a week
Weakened immune system with eye symptoms
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Tonsillitis.description: Tonsillitis is inflammation of the tonsils, two oval-shaped pads of tissue at the back of the throat. It commonly affects children but can occur at any age. Most cases are caused by common viruses.
common_symptoms: White or yellow coating on tonsils
Enlarged tender lymph nodes in neck
prevention: Don't share utensils or drinking cups
Avoid close contact with sick individuals
Boost immune system with good nutrition
home_remedies: Gargle with warm salt water multiple times daily
Drink warm liquids (tea with honey, broth)
Cold treats (popsicles, ice chips) to numb throat
Honey and lemon in warm water
Throat lozenges (adults and older children only)
general_advice: Pain relievers (acetaminophen, ibuprofen)
Antibiotics for bacterial tonsillitis (complete the full course)
Tonsillectomy may be recommended for recurrent tonsillitis
Rest is important for recovery
Soft foods are easier to swallow
when_to_see_doctor: Sore throat lasting more than 2 days
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Ear Infection.description: Ear infections (otitis media) occur when the middle ear becomes inflamed and infected. They are very common in children and can cause pain, hearing difficulties, and fever.
prevention: Keep up to date with vaccinations
Don't use cotton swabs inside ear canal
home_remedies: Warm compress on the affected ear
Over-the-counter ear drops for pain (if eardrum is intact)
Garlic oil drops (warm — check with doctor first)
Saline nasal spray to reduce congestion
general_advice: Pain management with acetaminophen or ibuprofen
Many ear infections resolve without antibiotics
Antibiotics prescribed for severe or bacterial infections
Ear tubes may be recommended for chronic ear infections
Follow-up hearing test after severe infections
when_to_see_doctor: Ear pain lasting more than a day
Symptoms in a child under 6 months
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Sciatica.description: Sciatica refers to pain that radiates along the path of the sciatic nerve, which extends from the lower back through the hips, buttocks, and down each leg. It typically affects only one side of the body.
common_symptoms: Pain radiating from lower back to buttock and down the leg
Numbness, tingling, or muscle weakness in the affected leg
prevention: Use proper body mechanics when lifting
general_advice: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain and inflammation
Muscle relaxants if prescribed
Physical therapy is highly effective
Epidural steroid injections for severe cases
Surgery (microdiscectomy) only if conservative treatment fails
Most cases resolve within 4-6 weeks
when_to_see_doctor: Pain not improving after a week of self-care
Sudden severe pain in low back or leg
Loss of bladder or bowel control (emergency)
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Carpal Tunnel Syndrome.description: Carpal tunnel syndrome is a condition caused by compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel in the wrist. It causes numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hand.
common_symptoms: Numbness and tingling in thumb, index, middle, and ring fingers
Weakness in hand and tendency to drop objects
Burning or itching sensation in palm
prevention: Take frequent breaks from repetitive activities
Keep wrists in neutral position while typing
home_remedies: Ice packs on wrist for 15-20 minutes
Take breaks every 30 minutes from repetitive tasks
general_advice: Wrist splints (especially at night) are first-line treatment
NSAIDs for pain and inflammation
Corticosteroid injections for temporary relief
Physical or occupational therapy
Surgery (carpal tunnel release) for severe cases
Modify activities that aggravate symptoms
when_to_see_doctor: Persistent numbness or tingling in fingers
Weakness in hand or dropping objects frequently
Symptoms interfering with daily activities or sleep
Inability to pinch between thumb and finger
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Depression.description: Depression (major depressive disorder) is a common but serious mood disorder that affects how you feel, think, and handle daily activities. It causes persistent feelings of sadness and loss of interest.
common_symptoms: Loss of interest in activities once enjoyed
prevention: Seek help early when feeling down
home_remedies: Spend time in nature and sunlight
Limit screen time and social media
general_advice: Psychotherapy (CBT, interpersonal therapy) is highly effective
Antidepressant medications may be prescribed
Combination of therapy and medication often works best
Don't stop medication without doctor's guidance
Recovery takes time — be patient with yourself
Support groups can be helpful
when_to_see_doctor: Thoughts of self-harm or suicide (seek immediate help)
Using alcohol or drugs to cope
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Anxiety Disorder.description: Anxiety disorders involve excessive, persistent worry and fear about everyday situations. They can interfere with daily activities, are difficult to control, and may include panic attacks.
common_symptoms: Panic attacks (chest pain, shortness of breath, dizziness)
general_advice: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is the gold standard
Anti-anxiety medications (SSRIs, SNRIs) may be prescribed
Benzodiazepines for short-term acute anxiety only
Beta-blockers for physical symptoms
Combination of therapy and lifestyle changes is most effective
Self-help and support groups can complement treatment
when_to_see_doctor: Difficulty maintaining relationships or work performance
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Insomnia.description: Insomnia is a common sleep disorder characterized by difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early and not being able to get back to sleep. It can affect energy, mood, health, and quality of life.
prevention: Exercise regularly (but not close to bedtime)
Avoid napping late in the day
home_remedies: Make bedroom dark, cool, and quiet
Warm milk or chamomile tea before bed
Meditation or deep breathing before sleep
general_advice: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) is most effective
Melatonin supplements (consult doctor for dosage)
Prescription sleep aids for short-term use only
Sleep restriction therapy
Stimulus control therapy
Address underlying conditions causing insomnia
when_to_see_doctor: Loud snoring with gasping (may indicate sleep apnea)
Excessive daytime sleepiness despite adequate time in bed
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Heat Stroke.description: Heat stroke is the most severe form of heat illness and is a medical emergency. It occurs when the body's temperature regulation fails and body temperature rises to 104°F (40°C) or higher.
causes: Strenuous physical activity in hot weather
common_symptoms: Hot, red, dry or damp skin
prevention: Stay hydrated (drink water before feeling thirsty)
Avoid outdoor activity during peak heat hours (10 AM - 4 PM)
Never leave anyone in a parked car
Take frequent breaks in shade or air conditioning
home_remedies: HEAT STROKE IS A MEDICAL EMERGENCY — CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES
Move person to a cool, shaded area immediately
Apply ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin
Fan the person while misting with cool water
If conscious, give small sips of cool water
Immerse in cool water if possible
general_advice: This is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment
IV fluids in hospital setting
Active cooling measures
Monitoring for organ damage
ICU care may be needed
Recovery may take days to weeks
Increased susceptibility to future heat illness after an episode
when_to_see_doctor: CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES IMMEDIATELY for heat stroke
Hot, dry skin with no sweating
Confusion, agitation, or loss of consciousness
Any altered mental state in hot weather
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.

Dehydration.description: Dehydration occurs when the body loses more fluid than it takes in. Even mild dehydration can drain energy and make you tired. Severe dehydration can be life-threatening, especially in children and older adults.
risk_factors: Working or exercising in hot weather
prevention: Drink water regularly throughout the day
Increase fluid intake in hot weather or during exercise
Monitor urine color (pale yellow is ideal)
Drink before, during, and after exercise
Replace fluids lost to vomiting or diarrhea promptly
home_remedies: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) — best for replacing lost electrolytes
Watermelon, cucumber, and oranges (high water content)
Buttermilk with a pinch of salt
Lemon water with salt and sugar
Small, frequent sips rather than gulping water
general_advice: Mild dehydration can be treated at home with fluids
ORS packets are available at pharmacies
Sports drinks can help replace electrolytes
Avoid sugary drinks and soda
IV fluids for moderate to severe dehydration
Address underlying cause (diarrhea, vomiting, etc.)
when_to_see_doctor: Signs of severe dehydration (extreme thirst, very dark urine, confusion)
Dehydration in infants, children, or elderly
clinical_treatments: Consult a healthcare professional for a personalized treatment plan.
Prescription medications may be required based on the severity and underlying cause.
Do not self-medicate with antibiotics or prescription drugs without medical supervision.
